title: Employees Whose Manager Left the Company
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: Pareto-optimal database challenge: the single SQL NOT EXISTS anti-lookup is also the speed extreme and memory extreme. Per the repo standard (no coincident files), only solution.sql is shipped; there is no separate solution-runtime or solution-memory file.

variants.languageReason: The task is a relational self anti-lookup with a simple salary filter and required primary-key ordering. SQL executes the filter and manager existence test inside the database engine, uses the primary key on employee_id, preserves native NULL semantics, and avoids client-side materialization; this is the fastest and leanest accepted interface for a LeetCode Database problem.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Not a LeetCode Database submission language for this schema; a client-side implementation would need to extract rows and build an id set before filtering.
Unsupported for this database prompt, and manual row handling would add transfer and memory overhead compared with a pushed-down SQL anti-lookup.
Strong for native in-memory membership checks, but unsupported here and dominated by executing the relational anti-join where the data resides.
Unsupported for this SQL schema submission; a map-based implementation would add application-side materialization and runtime/GC overhead.
Not the accepted query interface for the database track; managed row objects and maps would allocate more than the SQL plan.

Python/Pandas can express the same anti-lookup with an anti-merge or isin, but it materializes DataFrames; the other scripting languages are not the intended database submission path. SQL is leaner and optimizer-driven.

variants.approachJustification: Filter low-salary employees with a non-null manager_id, then keep only rows for which no manager row exists with employee_id equal to that manager_id; finally order by employee_id.
variants.timeComplexity: O(n) logical scan plus primary-key manager existence checks; O(r log r) sort only if the engine cannot satisfy ORDER BY from employee_id order

reasoningSummary: A manager who remains at the company is another row in Employees. A low-salary employee qualifies only when manager_id is non-null and no row exists for that manager id. NOT EXISTS states that anti-lookup directly and avoids unnecessary joins, grouping, or client-side state.
timeComplexity: O(n) logical scan plus primary-key manager existence checks; O(r log r) sort only if needed
